infobox.value: ([/waɪˈoʊmɪŋ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Wyoming.ogg "File:En-us-Wyoming.ogg") [*wy-OH-ming*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))[[9]](#cite_note-11) is a landlocked [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") in the [Mountain West](/wiki/Mountain_states "Mountain states") subregion of the [Western United States](/wiki/Western_United_States "Western United States"). It borders [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana") to the north and northwest, [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota") and [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska") to the east, [Idaho](/wiki/Idaho "Idaho") to the west, [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah") to the southwest, and [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado") to the south. With an estimated population of 587,618 as of 2024,[[2]](#cite_note-auto-3) Wyoming is the [least populous state](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_population "List of U.S. states and territories by population") despite being the [tenth-largest by area](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_area "List of U.S. states and territories by area"), and it has the [second-lowest population density](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_population_density "List of U.S. states by population density"). The [state capital](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_the_United_States "List of capitals in the United States") and [most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Wyoming "List of municipalities in Wyoming") is [Cheyenne](/wiki/Cheyenne,_Wyoming "Cheyenne, Wyoming").[[10]](#cite_note-QF-12)

**Wyoming** ([/waɪˈoʊmɪŋ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Wyoming.ogg "File:En-us-Wyoming.ogg") [*wy-OH-ming*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))[[9]](#cite_note-11) is a landlocked [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") in the [Mountain West](/wiki/Mountain_states "Mountain states") subregion of the [Western United States](/wiki/Western_United_States "Western United States"). It borders [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana") to the north and northwest, [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota") and [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska") to the east, [Idaho](/wiki/Idaho "Idaho") to the west, [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah") to the southwest, and [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado") to the south. With an estimated population of 587,618 as of 2024,[[2]](#cite_note-auto-3) Wyoming is the [least populous state](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_population "List of U.S. states and territories by population") despite being the [tenth-largest by area](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_area "List of U.S. states and territories by area"), and it has the [second-lowest population density](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_population_density "List of U.S. states by population density"). The [state capital](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_the_United_States "List of capitals in the United States") and [most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Wyoming "List of municipalities in Wyoming") is [Cheyenne](/wiki/Cheyenne,_Wyoming "Cheyenne, Wyoming").[[10]](#cite_note-QF-12)

Wyoming's western half consists mostly of the ranges and rangelands of the [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains"); its eastern half consists of high-elevation [prairie](/wiki/Prairie "Prairie"), and is referred to as the [High Plains](/wiki/High_Plains_(United_States) "High Plains (United States)"). Wyoming's climate is [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") in some parts and [continental](/wiki/Continental_climate "Continental climate") in others, making it drier and windier overall than other states, with greater temperature extremes. The [federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") owns just under half of Wyoming's land; the state ranks sixth in the amount of land—and fifth in the proportion of its land—that is owned by the federal government.[[11]](#cite_note-maineenvironment.org-13) Its federal lands include two national parks ([Grand Teton](/wiki/Grand_Teton_National_Park "Grand Teton National Park") and [Yellowstone](/wiki/Yellowstone_National_Park "Yellowstone National Park")), two national recreation areas, two national monuments, and several national forests, as well as historic sites, fish hatcheries, and wildlife refuges.

[Indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples "Indigenous peoples") have inhabited the region for thousands of years. Part of the land that became Wyoming came under American sovereignty via the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase"), part via the [Oregon Treaty](/wiki/Oregon_Treaty "Oregon Treaty"), and, lastly, via the [Mexican Cession](/wiki/Mexican_Cession "Mexican Cession"). With the opening of the [Oregon Trail](/wiki/Oregon_Trail "Oregon Trail"), the [Mormon Trail](/wiki/Mormon_Trail "Mormon Trail"), and the [California Trail](/wiki/California_Trail "California Trail"), vast numbers of pioneers traveled through parts of the state that had once been traversed mainly by fur trappers, and this spurred the establishment of forts, such as [Fort Laramie](/wiki/Fort_Laramie_National_Historic_Site "Fort Laramie National Historic Site"), that today serve as population centers.[[12]](#cite_note-14) The [Transcontinental Railroad](/wiki/First_transcontinental_railroad "First transcontinental railroad") supplanted the wagon trails in 1867 with a route through southern Wyoming,[[13]](#cite_note-15) bringing new settlers and the establishment of founding towns, including the state capital of Cheyenne.[[14]](#cite_note-16) On July 10, 1890, Wyoming became the union's 44th state.[[4]](#cite_note-WyoFacts-5) [Wyoming](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage_in_Wyoming "Women's suffrage in Wyoming") was the first state to allow women the [right to vote](/wiki/Suffrage "Suffrage") doing so since it was territory in 1869, and achieving statehood in 1890. It was also the first state to grant women the right to assume elected office, as well as the first state to elect a female governor, [Nellie Tayloe Ross](/wiki/Nellie_Tayloe_Ross "Nellie Tayloe Ross"). In honor of this part of its history, its official nickname is "The Equality State" and its official state motto is "Equal Rights".[[4]](#cite_note-WyoFacts-5)

Farming and ranching, and the attendant [range wars](/wiki/Range_war "Range war"), feature prominently in the state's history. Wyoming's economy is largely based on tourism and the extraction of minerals such as [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal"), [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas"), [oil](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum"), and [trona](/wiki/Trona "Trona"). Its agricultural commodities include barley, hay, livestock, [sugar beets](/wiki/Sugar_beet "Sugar beet"), wheat, and wool.

## Etymology

The region had acquired the name *Wyoming* by 1865 when Representative [James Mitchell Ashley](/wiki/James_Mitchell_Ashley "James Mitchell Ashley") of [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio") introduced a bill to Congress to provide a "temporary government for [the territory of Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming_Territory "Wyoming Territory")". The territory was named after the [Wyoming Valley](/wiki/Wyoming_Valley "Wyoming Valley") in [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"). [Thomas Campbell](/wiki/Thomas_Campbell_(poet) "Thomas Campbell (poet)") wrote his 1809 poem "[Gertrude of Wyoming](/wiki/Gertrude_of_Wyoming "Gertrude of Wyoming")", inspired by the [Battle of Wyoming](/wiki/Battle_of_Wyoming "Battle of Wyoming") in the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"). The name ultimately derives from the Lenape [Munsee](/wiki/Munsee_language "Munsee language") word *xwé:wamənk* ('at the big river flat').[[15]](#cite_note-Bright,_William_2004_pg._576-17)[[16]](#cite_note-State_of_Wyoming—Narrative-18)

## History

Main article: [History of Wyoming](/wiki/History_of_Wyoming "History of Wyoming")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Alfred_Jacob_Miller_-_Fort_Laramie_-_Walters_37194049.jpg/330px-Alfred_Jacob_Miller_-_Fort_Laramie_-_Walters_37194049.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alfred_Jacob_Miller_-_Fort_Laramie_-_Walters_37194049.jpg)

The first [Fort Laramie](/wiki/Fort_Laramie "Fort Laramie") as it looked before 1840 (painting from memory by Alfred Jacob Miller)

Several [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") groups originally inhabited the region today known as Wyoming. The [Crow](/wiki/Crow_Nation "Crow Nation"), [Arapaho](/wiki/Arapaho "Arapaho"), [Lakota](/wiki/Lakota_people "Lakota people"), and [Shoshone](/wiki/Shoshone "Shoshone") were but a few of the original inhabitants European explorers encountered when they first visited the region. What is now southwestern Wyoming was claimed by the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire"), which extended through the Southwest and Mexico. With Mexican independence in 1821, it was considered part of [Alta California](/wiki/Alta_California "Alta California"). U.S. expansion brought settlers who fought for control. Mexico ceded these territories after its defeat in 1848 in the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War").

From the late 18th century, [French-Canadian](/wiki/French-Canadian "French-Canadian") trappers from Québec and Montréal regularly entered the area for trade with the tribes. French toponyms such as Téton and [La Ramie](/wiki/Jacques_La_Ramee "Jacques La Ramee") are marks of that history.[[17]](#cite_note-:1-19)

American [John Colter](/wiki/John_Colter "John Colter") first recorded a description in English of the region in 1807. He was a member of the [Lewis and Clark Expedition](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_Expedition "Lewis and Clark Expedition"), which was guided by French Canadian [Toussaint Charbonneau](/wiki/Toussaint_Charbonneau "Toussaint Charbonneau") and his young Shoshone wife, [Sacagawea](/wiki/Sacagawea "Sacagawea"). At the time, Colter's reports of the [Yellowstone](/wiki/Greater_Yellowstone_Ecosystem "Greater Yellowstone Ecosystem") area were considered fictional.[[18]](#cite_note-20) On a return from [Astoria](/wiki/Astoria,_Oregon "Astoria, Oregon"), [Robert Stuart](/wiki/Robert_Stuart_(explorer) "Robert Stuart (explorer)") and a party of five men discovered [South Pass](/wiki/South_Pass_(Wyoming) "South Pass (Wyoming)") in 1812.[[17]](#cite_note-:1-19)

The [Oregon Trail](/wiki/Oregon_Trail "Oregon Trail") later followed that route as emigrants moved to the west coast. In 1850, mountain man [Jim Bridger](/wiki/Jim_Bridger "Jim Bridger") first documented what is now known as [Bridger Pass](/wiki/Bridger_Pass "Bridger Pass").[[19]](#cite_note-:2-21) Bridger also explored Yellowstone, and filed reports on the region that, like Colter's, were largely regarded at the time as [tall tales](/wiki/Tall_tale "Tall tale"). The [Union Pacific Railroad](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Railroad "Union Pacific Railroad") constructed track through Bridger Pass in 1868.[[19]](#cite_note-:2-21) It was used as the route for construction of [Interstate 80](/wiki/Interstate_80_in_Wyoming "Interstate 80 in Wyoming") through the mountains 90 years later.[[20]](#cite_note-22)

After the Union Pacific Railroad reached [Cheyenne](/wiki/Cheyenne,_Wyoming "Cheyenne, Wyoming") in 1867, population growth was stimulated. The federal government established the [Wyoming Territory](/wiki/Wyoming_Territory "Wyoming Territory") on July 25, 1868.[[21]](#cite_note-23) Lacking significant deposits of gold and silver, unlike mineral-rich [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), Wyoming did not have such a population boom. But [South Pass City](/wiki/South_Pass_City,_Wyoming "South Pass City, Wyoming") had a short-lived boom after the Carissa Mine began producing gold in 1867.[[22]](#cite_note-24) Copper was mined in some areas between the [Sierra Madre Mountains](/wiki/Sierra_Madre_Range_(Wyoming) "Sierra Madre Range (Wyoming)") and the Snowy Range near [Grand Encampment](/wiki/Grand_Encampment,_Wyoming "Grand Encampment, Wyoming").[[23]](#cite_note-25)

Once government-sponsored expeditions to the Yellowstone country began, Colter's and Bridger's descriptions of the region's landscape were confirmed. In 1872, [Yellowstone National Park](/wiki/Yellowstone_National_Park "Yellowstone National Park") was created as the world's first, to protect this area. Nearly all of the park lies within the northwestern corner of Wyoming.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/3c_Wyoming_Statehood_50th_anniversary%2C_1940_issue.jpg/250px-3c_Wyoming_Statehood_50th_anniversary%2C_1940_issue.jpg)](/wiki/File:3c_Wyoming_Statehood_50th_anniversary,_1940_issue.jpg)

On July 10, 1940, the U.S. Post Office issued a postage stamp commemorating the 50th anniversary of Wyoming statehood. The engraving depicts the Wyoming state seal, which features a central figure of a woman in front of a banner reading 'equal rights'

On December 10, 1869, territorial Governor [John Allen Campbell](/wiki/John_Allen_Campbell "John Allen Campbell") extended the right to vote to women, making Wyoming the first territory to do so, and upon statehood became the first state to grant [women's suffrage](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage "Women's suffrage").[[24]](#cite_note-26) Women first served on juries in Wyoming ([Laramie](/wiki/Laramie,_Wyoming "Laramie, Wyoming") in 1870). Wyoming was also a pioneer in welcoming women into electoral politics.[[25]](#cite_note-Helton-27) It had the first female court bailiff (Mary Atkinson, Laramie, in 1870), and the first female [justice of the peace](/wiki/Justice_of_the_peace "Justice of the peace") in the country ([Esther Hobart Morris](/wiki/Esther_Hobart_Morris "Esther Hobart Morris"), South Pass City, in 1870). In 1924, Wyoming was the first state to elect a female governor, [Nellie Tayloe Ross](/wiki/Nellie_Tayloe_Ross "Nellie Tayloe Ross"), who took office in January 1925.[[26]](#cite_note-28) Due to its civil-rights history, one of Wyoming's state nicknames is "The Equality State", and the official state motto is "Equal Rights".[[4]](#cite_note-WyoFacts-5)

Wyoming's constitution also included a pioneering article on [water rights](/wiki/Water_right "Water right").[[27]](#cite_note-29) Bills for Wyoming Territory's admission to the union were introduced in both the [U.S. Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") and [U.S. House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") in December 1889. On March 27, 1890, the House passed the bill and President [Benjamin Harrison](/wiki/Benjamin_Harrison "Benjamin Harrison") signed Wyoming's statehood bill; Wyoming became the 44th state in the union on July 10, 1890.[[4]](#cite_note-WyoFacts-5)

Wyoming was the location of the [Johnson County War](/wiki/Johnson_County_War "Johnson County War") of 1892, which erupted between competing groups of cattle ranchers. The passage of the [Homestead Act](/wiki/Homestead_Act "Homestead Act") led to an influx of small ranchers. A [range war](/wiki/Range_war "Range war") broke out when either or both of the groups chose violent conflict over commercial competition in the use of the public land.

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Wyoming](/wiki/Geography_of_Wyoming "Geography of Wyoming")

### Climate

Further information: [Climate change in Wyoming](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Wyoming "Climate change in Wyoming")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Types_Wyoming.png/250px-K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Types_Wyoming.png)](/wiki/File:K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Types_Wyoming.png)

[Köppen climate types](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") of Wyoming, using 1991–2020 [climate normals](/wiki/Climatological_normal "Climatological normal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Autumn_in_the_Bighorn_Mountains.JPG/250px-Autumn_in_the_Bighorn_Mountains.JPG)](/wiki/File:Autumn_in_the_Bighorn_Mountains.JPG)

Autumn in the Bighorn Mountains

Wyoming's climate is generally [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") and [continental](/wiki/Continental_climate "Continental climate") ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *[*BSk*](/wiki/Cold_steppe "Cold steppe")*) and is drier and windier in comparison to most of the United States with greater temperature extremes.[[28]](#cite_note-30)[[29]](#cite_note-31) Much of this is due to the topography of the state. Summers in Wyoming are warm with July high temperatures averaging between 80 and 90 °F (27 and 32 °C) in most of the state. With increasing elevation, however, this average drops rapidly with locations above 9,000 feet (2,700 m) averaging around 70 °F (21 °C). Summer nights throughout the state are characterized by a rapid cooldown with even the hottest locations averaging in the 50–60 °F (10–16 °C) range at night. In most of the state, most of the precipitation tends to fall in the late spring and early summer. Winters are cold but are variable with periods of sometimes extreme cold interspersed between generally mild periods, with [Chinook winds](/wiki/Chinook_winds "Chinook winds") providing unusually warm temperatures in some locations.

Wyoming is a dry state with much of the land receiving less than 10 inches (250 mm) of rainfall per year. Precipitation depends on elevation with lower areas in the [Big Horn Basin](/wiki/Bighorn_Basin "Bighorn Basin") averaging 5–8 inches (130–200 mm), making the area nearly a true [desert](/wiki/Desert "Desert"). The lower areas in the North and on the eastern plains typically average around 10–12 inches (250–300 mm), making the climate there [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid "Semi-arid"). Some mountain areas do receive a good amount of precipitation, 20 inches (510 mm) or more, much of it as snow, sometimes 200 inches (510 cm) or more annually. The state's highest recorded temperature is 114 °F (46 °C) at [Basin](/wiki/Basin,_Wyoming "Basin, Wyoming") on July 12, 1900, and the lowest recorded temperature is −66 °F (−54 °C) at [Riverside](/wiki/Riverside,_Wyoming "Riverside, Wyoming") on February 9, 1933.

The number of [thunderstorm](/wiki/Thunderstorm "Thunderstorm") days varies across the state with the southeastern plains of the state having the most days of thunderstorm activity. Thunderstorm activity in the state is highest during the late spring and early summer. The southeastern corner of the state is the most vulnerable part of the state to [tornado](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado") activity. Moving away from that point and westwards, the incidence of tornadoes drops dramatically with the west part of the state showing little vulnerability. Tornadoes, where they occur, tend to be small and brief, unlike some of those that occur farther east.

### Location and size

As specified in the designating legislation for the [Territory of Wyoming](/wiki/Territory_of_Wyoming "Territory of Wyoming"), Wyoming's borders are lines of [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") [41°N](/wiki/41st_parallel_north "41st parallel north") and [45°N](/wiki/45th_parallel_north "45th parallel north"), and [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") 104°3'W and 111°3'W (27 and 34 west of the [Washington Meridian](/wiki/Washington_Meridian "Washington Meridian")) –a [geodesic](/wiki/Geodesic "Geodesic") quadrangle.[[35]](#cite_note-37) Wyoming is one of only three states (the others being [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado") and [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah")) to have borders defined by *only* "straight" lines. Due to surveying inaccuracies during the 19th century, Wyoming's legal border deviates from the true [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") and [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") lines by up to one-half mile (0.80 km) in some spots, especially in the mountainous region along the [45th parallel](/wiki/45th_parallel_north "45th parallel north").[[36]](#cite_note-38) Wyoming is bordered on the north by [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana"), on the east by [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota") and [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska"), on the south by [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), on the southwest by [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah"), and on the west by [Idaho](/wiki/Idaho "Idaho"). It is the tenth largest state in the United States in total area, containing 97,814 square miles (253,340 km2) and is made up of 23 counties. From the north border to the south border, it is 276 miles (444 km); and from the east to the west border is 365 miles (587 km) at its south end and 342 miles (550 km) at the north end.

### Natural landforms

#### Mountain ranges

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Barns_grand_tetons.jpg/250px-Barns_grand_tetons.jpg)](/wiki/File:Barns_grand_tetons.jpg)

[Teton Range](/wiki/Teton_Range "Teton Range")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Cattle_Drive_near_Pinedale%2C_WY_%2814963962303%29.jpg/250px-Cattle_Drive_near_Pinedale%2C_WY_%2814963962303%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cattle_Drive_near_Pinedale,_WY_(14963962303).jpg)

[Green River](/wiki/Green_River_(Colorado_River_tributary) "Green River (Colorado River tributary)") valley

The [Great Plains](/wiki/Great_Plains "Great Plains") meet the [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains") in Wyoming. The state is a great [plateau](/wiki/Plateau "Plateau") broken by many [mountain ranges](/wiki/Mountain_range "Mountain range"). Surface elevations range from the summit of [Gannett Peak](/wiki/Gannett_Peak "Gannett Peak") in the [Wind River Mountain Range](/wiki/Wind_River_Range "Wind River Range"), at 13,804 feet (4,207 m), to the [Belle Fourche River](/wiki/Belle_Fourche_River "Belle Fourche River") valley in the state's northeast corner, at 3,125 feet (952 m). In the northwest are the [Absaroka](/wiki/Absaroka_Range "Absaroka Range"), [Owl Creek](/wiki/Owl_Creek_Mountains "Owl Creek Mountains"), [Gros Ventre](/wiki/Gros_Ventre_Range "Gros Ventre Range"), [Wind River](/wiki/Wind_River_Range "Wind River Range"), and the [Teton](/wiki/Teton_Range "Teton Range") ranges. In the north central are the [Big Horn Mountains](/wiki/Big_Horn_Mountains "Big Horn Mountains"); in the northeast, the [Black Hills](/wiki/Black_Hills "Black Hills"); and in the southern region the [Laramie](/wiki/Laramie_Mountains "Laramie Mountains"), [Snowy](/wiki/Medicine_Bow_Mountains "Medicine Bow Mountains"), and [Sierra Madre](/wiki/Sierra_Madre_Range_(Wyoming) "Sierra Madre Range (Wyoming)") ranges.

The Snowy Range in the south-central part of the state is an extension of the Colorado [Rockies](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains") both in geology and in appearance. The Wind River Range in the west central part of the state is remote and includes more than 40 mountain peaks in excess of 13,000 ft (4,000 m) tall in addition to [Gannett Peak](/wiki/Gannett_Peak "Gannett Peak"), the highest peak in the state. The Bighorn Mountains in the north-central portion are somewhat isolated from the bulk of the Rocky Mountains.

The Teton Range in the northwest extends for 50 miles (80 km), part of which is included in [Grand Teton National Park](/wiki/Grand_Teton_National_Park "Grand Teton National Park"). The park includes the [Grand Teton](/wiki/Grand_Teton "Grand Teton"), the second-highest peak in the state.

The [Continental Divide](/wiki/Continental_Divide "Continental Divide") spans north–south across the central portion of the state. Rivers east of the divide drain into the [Missouri River Basin](/wiki/Missouri_River_Basin "Missouri River Basin") and eventually the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico"). They are the [North Platte](/wiki/North_Platte_River "North Platte River"), [Wind](/wiki/Wind_River_(Wyoming) "Wind River (Wyoming)"), [Bighorn](/wiki/Bighorn_River "Bighorn River"), and [Yellowstone](/wiki/Yellowstone_River "Yellowstone River") rivers. The [Snake River](/wiki/Snake_River "Snake River") in northwest Wyoming eventually drains into the [Columbia River](/wiki/Columbia_River "Columbia River") and the Pacific Ocean, as does the [Green River](/wiki/Green_River_(Colorado_River) "Green River (Colorado River)") through the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River") Basin.

The Continental Divide forks in the south-central part of the state in an area known as the [Great Divide Basin](/wiki/Great_Divide_Basin "Great Divide Basin") where water that precipitates onto or flows into it cannot reach an ocean—it *all* sinks into the soil and eventually evaporates.

Several rivers begin in or flow through the state, including the Yellowstone River, Bighorn River, Green River, and the Snake River.

#### Basins

Much of Wyoming is covered with large basins containing different eco-regions, from shrublands to smaller patches of desert.[[37]](#cite_note-Wyo-basins-39) Regions of the state classified as basins contain everything from large geologic formations to sand dunes and vast unpopulated spaces.[[38]](#cite_note-Ecoregions-Wyoming-40) Basin landscapes are typically at lower elevations and include rolling hills, valleys, mesas, terraces and other rugged terrain, but also include natural springs as well as rivers and artificial reservoirs.[[39]](#cite_note-epaEcoregionsofWyoming-41) They have common plant species such as various subspecies of [sagebrush](/wiki/Artemisia_tridentata "Artemisia tridentata"), [juniper](/wiki/Juniper "Juniper") and grasses such as [wheatgrass](/wiki/Agropyron "Agropyron"), but basins are known for their diversity of plant and animal species.[[37]](#cite_note-Wyo-basins-39)

#### Islands

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of islands of Wyoming](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Wyoming "List of islands of Wyoming").

Wyoming has 32 named islands; the majority are in [Jackson Lake](/wiki/Jackson_Lake_(Wyoming) "Jackson Lake (Wyoming)") and [Yellowstone Lake](/wiki/Yellowstone_Lake "Yellowstone Lake"), within [Yellowstone National Park](/wiki/Yellowstone_National_Park "Yellowstone National Park") in the northwest portion of the state. The [Green River](/wiki/Green_River_(Colorado_River) "Green River (Colorado River)") in the southwest also contains a number of islands.

### Population

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Wyoming_population_density_2020.png/500px-Wyoming_population_density_2020.png)](/wiki/File:Wyoming_population_density_2020.png)

The largest population centers are [Cheyenne](/wiki/Cheyenne,_WY_MSA "Cheyenne, WY MSA") (southeast) and [Casper](/wiki/Casper,_WY,_MSA "Casper, WY, MSA").

The [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census") counted 576,851 people living in Wyoming.[[44]](#cite_note-:3-47) The [center of population](/wiki/Center_of_population "Center of population") of Wyoming is in [Natrona County](/wiki/Natrona_County,_Wyoming "Natrona County, Wyoming").[[45]](#cite_note-48)[[46]](#cite_note-49) Sparsely populated, Wyoming is the least populous state of the United States. Wyoming has the second-lowest population density in the country (behind [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska")) and is the sparsest-populated of the 48 [contiguous states](/wiki/Contiguous_states "Contiguous states"). It is one of only two states with a population smaller than that of the nation's capital; the only other state with this distinction is [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont").

According to [HUD](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Housing_and_Urban_Development "United States Department of Housing and Urban Development")'s 2022 [Annual Homeless Assessment Report](/wiki/Annual_Homeless_Assessment_Report_to_Congress "Annual Homeless Assessment Report to Congress"), there were an estimated 648 [homeless](/wiki/Homelessness "Homelessness") people in Wyoming.[[47]](#cite_note-50)[[48]](#cite_note-51)

According to the 2020 census, the population's racial composition was 84.7% [white](/wiki/White_American "White American") (81.4% non-Hispanic white), 2.4% American Indian and Alaska Native, 0.9% Black or African American, 0.9% Asian American, and 0.1% Native Hawaiian or Pacific Islander, 3.5% from some other race, and 7.5% from two or more races.[[49]](#cite_note-2020DP1-52) As of 2011, 24.9% of Wyoming's population younger than age 1 were minorities.[[50]](#cite_note-53) According to data from the [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey"), as of 2018, Wyoming was the only U.S. state where African Americans earn a higher median income than white workers.[[51]](#cite_note-54)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Ethnic_Origins_in_Wyoming.png/250px-Ethnic_Origins_in_Wyoming.png)](/wiki/File:Ethnic_Origins_in_Wyoming.png)

Ethnic origins in Wyoming

As of 2015, Wyoming had an estimated population of 586,107, which was an increase of 1,954, or 0.29%, from the prior year and an increase of 22,481, or 3.99%, since the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_Census "2010 United States Census"). This includes a natural increase since the last census of 12,165 (33,704 births minus 21,539 deaths) and an increase from net migration of 4,035 into the state. Immigration resulted in a net increase of 2,264 and migration within the country produced a net increase of 1,771. In 2004, the foreign-born population was 11,000 (2.2%). In 2005, total births in Wyoming were 7,231 (birth rate of 14.04 per thousand).[[52]](#cite_note-55)

Wyoming experienced its first population decline since 1990, with a decrease of just over 1,000 people (0.2 percent) from July 2015 to July 2016. This decline was attributed to the downturn in the state's mineral extraction industry, particularly the oil and gas sector, which led to the loss of thousands of jobs. However, state economist Jim Robinson noted signs of economic stabilization. Job losses in the oil and gas industry appeared to have leveled off, and there was a slight increase in drilling activity in recent months. While the state's economy showed little growth, it was considered to have reached a more stable condition as of late 2016.[[53]](#cite_note-56)

According to the 2000 census, the largest ancestry groups in Wyoming were: [German](/wiki/German-American "German-American") (25.9%), [English](/wiki/English_American "English American") (15.9%), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_American "Irish American") (13.3%), and [American Indian](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") (4.7%). An additional 6.4% responded with "American" as their ancestry.[[54]](#cite_note-57)

In 2018, the top countries of origin for Wyoming's immigrants were [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), [China](/wiki/China "China"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), [England](/wiki/England "England") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada").[[55]](#cite_note-58)

### Languages

See also: [Native American languages of Wyoming](/wiki/Native_American_languages_of_Wyoming "Native American languages of Wyoming")

In 2010, 93.39% (474,343) of Wyomingites over age 5 spoke [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") as their [primary language](/wiki/Primary_language "Primary language"); 4.47% (22,722) spoke [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), 0.35% (1,771) spoke [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), and 0.28% (1,434) spoke [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"). Other common non-English languages included [Algonquian](/wiki/Algonquian_languages "Algonquian languages") (0.18%), [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") (0.10%), [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language"), and [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") (both 0.09%).[[67]](#cite_note-70)

In 2007, the [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") reported 6.2% (30,419) of Wyoming's population over five spoke a language other than English at home. Of those, 68.1% were able to speak English very well, 16.0% spoke English well, 10.9% did not speak English well, and 5.0% did not speak English at all.[[68]](#cite_note-Language2007-71)

### Religion

Religious self-identification, [Public Religion Research Institute](/wiki/Public_Religion_Research_Institute "Public Religion Research Institute")'s 2020 *American Values Atlas* survey.[[69]](#cite_note-:0-72)

1. [Unaffiliated](/wiki/Irreligion_in_the_United_States "Irreligion in the United States") (40.0%)
2. [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism_in_the_United_States "Protestantism in the United States") (33.0%)
3. [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism_in_the_United_States "Catholicism in the United States") (15.0%)
4. [Mormonism](/wiki/Mormonism_in_the_United_States "Mormonism in the United States") (7.00%)
5. [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism_in_the_United_States "Judaism in the United States") (1.00%)
6. Other (4.00%)

In 2020, the [Public Religion Research Institute](/wiki/Public_Religion_Research_Institute "Public Religion Research Institute") determined that about 55% of Wyoming's adult population was [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), primarily [evangelical](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism") and [mainline Protestant](/wiki/Mainline_Protestant "Mainline Protestant"), [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), and [Mormon](/wiki/Mormonism "Mormonism"). The Public Religion Research Institute survey documented a decrease in religiosity from a 2014 separate [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") study;[[70]](#cite_note-pew2014-73) according to the Public Religion Research Institute, the unaffiliated made up 40% of the state population by 2020. According to a 2013 Gallup poll, Wyomingites' religious affiliations were 49% Protestant, 23% [nonreligious](/wiki/Nonreligious "Nonreligious") or other, 18% Catholic, 9% [Latter-day Saint](/wiki/Latter_Day_Saint_movement "Latter Day Saint movement") (Mormons), and less than 1% [Jewish](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism").[[71]](#cite_note-74)

A 2010 [Association of Religion Data Archives](/wiki/Association_of_Religion_Data_Archives "Association of Religion Data Archives") (ARDA) report recognized as Wyoming's largest denominations [the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") (LDS Church), with 62,804 (11%); the Catholic Church, with 61,222 (10.8%); and the [Southern Baptist Convention](/wiki/Southern_Baptist_Convention "Southern Baptist Convention"), with 15,812 (2.8%). The report counted 59,247 evangelical Protestants (10.5%), 36,539 mainline Protestants (6.5%), 785 Eastern Orthodox Christians; 281 Black Protestants; 65,000 adhering to other traditions; and 340,552 claiming no religious tradition.[[72]](#cite_note-75) In 2020, ARDA reported the state's largest individual denominations as the following: the Catholic Church (69,500); the LDS Church (67,729); and the Southern Baptist Convention (11,082). Non-denominational Protestants were 23,410 in number.[[73]](#cite_note-76)

According to ARDA's 2020 report, the Roman Catholics had an adherence rate of 120.48 per 1,000 people, Mormons 117.41 per 1,000 people, and Southern Baptists 19.21 per 1,000 people. Non-denominational Protestants had an adherence rate of 40.58 per 1,000 people; these trends reflected the separate 2014 Pew study's varying attendance at religious services. In 2014, 38% visited a religious service at least once a week, 28% once or twice a month, and 32% seldom/never.[[70]](#cite_note-pew2014-73) A 2018 research article by the [National Christian Foundation](/wiki/National_Christian_Foundation "National Christian Foundation") cited non-churchgoing Christians nationwide did not attend religious services often through practicing the faith in other ways, not finding a house of worship they liked, disliking sermons and feeling unwelcomed, and logistics.[[74]](#cite_note-77)

## Economy and infrastructure

Further information: [List of Wyoming locations by per capita income](/wiki/List_of_Wyoming_locations_by_per_capita_income "List of Wyoming locations by per capita income") and [List of power stations in Wyoming](/wiki/List_of_power_stations_in_Wyoming "List of power stations in Wyoming")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Wind_Power_4892597382.jpg/250px-Wind_Power_4892597382.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wind_Power_4892597382.jpg)

[Wind farm](/wiki/Wind_farm "Wind farm") in [Uinta County](/wiki/Uinta_County,_Wyoming "Uinta County, Wyoming")

According to the [Bureau of Economic Analysis](/wiki/Bureau_of_Economic_Analysis "Bureau of Economic Analysis"), Wyoming's [gross state product](/wiki/Gross_regional_domestic_product "Gross regional domestic product") in 2024 was [$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")52.9 billion.[[75]](#cite_note-78) As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wyoming&action=edit), the population was growing slightly with the most growth in tourist-oriented areas, such as [Teton County](/wiki/Teton_County,_Wyoming "Teton County, Wyoming"). Boom conditions in neighboring states, such as [North Dakota](/wiki/North_Dakota "North Dakota"), were drawing energy workers away. About half of Wyoming's counties showed population loss.[[76]](#cite_note-CST032915-79) The state makes active efforts through Wyoming Grown, an internet-based recruitment program, to find jobs for young people educated in Wyoming who have emigrated but may wish to return.[[77]](#cite_note-NYT71515-80) As of May 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wyoming&action=edit), the state's [unemployment rate](/wiki/Unemployment "Unemployment") was 3.3%.[[78]](#cite_note-81)

In 2025, Wyoming experienced its highest growth in new business formations, up 35% from 2024 with 227,723 new businesses formed, according to [Business Observer](/wiki/Business_Observer "Business Observer").[[79]](#cite_note-82) That same year, [small businesses](/wiki/Small_business "Small business") made up 98.9% of businesses in the state, and employed 65.2% of its work force.[[80]](#cite_note-83)

The mineral-extraction industry and travel and tourism sector are the main drivers of Wyoming's economy.[[81]](#cite_note-aboutwyogov-84) The federal government owns about 42.3% of its landmass, while the state controls 6%.[[81]](#cite_note-aboutwyogov-84) The total taxable value of mining production in Wyoming in 2007 was over $14.5 billion. In 2018, the [tourism industry](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism") contributed approximately $3.8 billion in spending from domestic and international visitors.[[81]](#cite_note-aboutwyogov-84)

In 2002, more than six million people visited Wyoming's [national parks](/wiki/National_park "National park") and monuments. Wyoming's main tourist attractions include [Grand Teton National Park](/wiki/Grand_Teton_National_Park "Grand Teton National Park"), [Yellowstone National Park](/wiki/Yellowstone_National_Park "Yellowstone National Park"), [Devils Tower National Monument](/wiki/Devils_Tower_National_Monument "Devils Tower National Monument"), [Independence Rock](/wiki/Independence_Rock_(Wyoming) "Independence Rock (Wyoming)") and [Fossil Butte National Monument](/wiki/Fossil_Butte_National_Monument "Fossil Butte National Monument"). Yellowstone, established in 1872 as the world's first national park, attracts over three million visitors each year.

Historically, agriculture has been an important component of Wyoming's economy. Its overall importance to the economy has waned, but it is still an essential part of Wyoming's culture and lifestyle. The main agricultural commodities Wyoming produces include livestock (beef), [hay](/wiki/Hay "Hay"), [sugar beets](/wiki/Sugar_beets "Sugar beets"), grain (wheat and barley), and [wool](/wiki/Wool "Wool"). More than 91% of Wyoming's land is classified as rural.

Wyoming is the home of only a handful of companies with a regional or national presence. [Taco John's](/wiki/Taco_John%27s "Taco John's") and [Sierra Trading Post](/wiki/Sierra_Trading_Post "Sierra Trading Post"), both in Cheyenne, are privately held. [Cloud Peak Energy](/wiki/Cloud_Peak_Energy "Cloud Peak Energy") in Gillette and U.S. Energy Corp. (NASDAQ: USEG) in Riverton are Wyoming's only publicly traded companies.

Various initiatives have been put in place and legislation adopted to encourage the use of blockchain technology and cryptocurrencies in the state.[[82]](#cite_note-85) [Tyler Lindholm](/wiki/Tyler_Lindholm "Tyler Lindholm"), a former state legislator, claimed that 500 member-owned limited liability companies built on blockchain had been established and that 17,000 businesses with "crypto" in their name were registered by 2023.
State legislators appointed a commission in 2023 to create a [stablecoin](/wiki/Stablecoin "Stablecoin"), aiming to be the first cryptocurrency created by a U.S. state.[[83]](#cite_note-86)[[84]](#cite_note-87)[[85]](#cite_note-88) It launched in August 2025.[[86]](#cite_note-89)

### Mineral and energy production

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Wyoming_Coal_production.png/250px-Wyoming_Coal_production.png)](/wiki/File:Wyoming_Coal_production.png)

Wyoming coal production from 2001 to 2023.[[87]](#cite_note-wyocoal-prodgraph-90)

[North Antelope Rochelle Mine](/wiki/North_Antelope_Rochelle_Mine "North Antelope Rochelle Mine"), the largest estimated coal mine reserve in the world, as of 2013[[88]](#cite_note-91)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Rig_wind_river.jpg/250px-Rig_wind_river.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rig_wind_river.jpg)

A natural gas [rig](/wiki/Drilling_rig "Drilling rig") west of the [Wind River Range](/wiki/Wind_River_Range "Wind River Range")

Wyoming's mineral commodities include coal, [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas"), [coalbed methane](/wiki/Coalbed_methane "Coalbed methane"), [crude oil](/wiki/Crude_oil "Crude oil"), [uranium](/wiki/Uranium "Uranium"), and [trona](/wiki/Trona "Trona").

#### Coal

Wyoming produced 277 million [short tons](/wiki/Short_ton "Short ton") (251 million metric tons) of coal in 2019, a 9% drop from 2018.[[89]](#cite_note-wyocoal-prod-92) [Wyoming's coal production](/wiki/Coal_mining_in_Wyoming "Coal mining in Wyoming") peaked in 2008, when 514 million short tons (466 million metric tons) were produced.[[89]](#cite_note-wyocoal-prod-92) Wyoming has a reserve of 68.7 billion tons (62.3 billion metric tons) of coal. Major coal areas include the [Powder River Basin](/wiki/Powder_River_Basin "Powder River Basin") and the [Green River Basin](/wiki/Green_River_Basin "Green River Basin"). In 2002, [coalbed methane extraction](/wiki/Coalbed_methane_extraction "Coalbed methane extraction") (CBM), a method for the extracting of methane, yielded 327.5 billion cubic feet (9.3 km3).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 2016, Wyoming produced 1.77 trillion cubic feet (50 billion m3) of natural gas, ranking the state sixth nationwide in natural gas production.[[90]](#cite_note-93)

#### Oil

Wyoming produced 53.4 million barrels (8.49×10~~^~~6 m3) of crude oil in 2007, ranking fifth nationwide in oil production. By 2022, Wyoming ranked eighth nationally in the production of both crude oil and natural gas and was the second-largest producer of oil and gas on federal lands. At its peak in 2022, the state had 27,951 producing wells, including 10,120 oil wells and 17,800 gas wells. Wyoming's oil reserves were estimated at 978 million barrels at the end of 2021, representing 2.4% of U.S. reserves. The state had four operational refineries in 2022 with a combined refining capacity of 125,850 barrels per day, a significant reduction from the 14 refineries operating in 1981.[[91]](#cite_note-petro-94)[[92]](#cite_note-pawyo_facts-95)

#### Wind energy

Further information: [Wind power in Wyoming](/wiki/Wind_power_in_Wyoming "Wind power in Wyoming")

Because of its geography and altitude, the potential for wind energy in Wyoming is one of the highest of any U.S. state. The [Chokecherry and Sierra Madre Wind Energy Project](/wiki/Chokecherry_and_Sierra_Madre_Wind_Energy_Project "Chokecherry and Sierra Madre Wind Energy Project") is the largest commercial wind generation facility under development in North America.[[93]](#cite_note-96) [Carbon County](/wiki/Carbon_County,_Wyoming "Carbon County, Wyoming") is home to the largest proposed wind farm in the nation. Construction plans have been halted because of proposed new taxes on wind power energy production.[[94]](#cite_note-97)

#### Other

The [Kelsey Lake Diamond Mine](/wiki/Kelsey_Lake_Diamond_Mine "Kelsey Lake Diamond Mine") in Colorado, less than 1,000 feet (300 m) from the Wyoming border, produced gem-quality diamonds for several years.[[95]](#cite_note-98)[[96]](#cite_note-99) The [Wyoming craton](/wiki/Wyoming_craton "Wyoming craton"), which hosts the [kimberlite](/wiki/Kimberlite "Kimberlite") [volcanic pipes](/wiki/Volcanic_pipe "Volcanic pipe") that were mined, underlies most of Wyoming.
Wyoming possesses the world's largest known reserve of [trona](/wiki/Trona "Trona"),[[97]](#cite_note-gearino-100) a mineral used in manufacturing glass, paper, soaps, baking soda, water softeners, and pharmaceuticals. In 2008, Wyoming produced 46 million short tons (41.7 million metric tons) of trona, 25% of the world's production.[[97]](#cite_note-gearino-100)
Although [uranium mining in Wyoming](/wiki/Uranium_mining_in_Wyoming "Uranium mining in Wyoming") is much less active than in previous decades, a sharp [rise in uranium prices](/wiki/Uranium_bubble_of_2007 "Uranium bubble of 2007") in 2007 spurred new interest in prospecting and mining.[[98]](#cite_note-101) In 2024, the uranium industry in the state experienced a significant resurgence due to a sharp increase in uranium prices.[[99]](#cite_note-102) Rare earth metals are also among Wyoming's mineral commodities.[[100]](#cite_note-103)

### Taxes

Unlike most other states, Wyoming levies no individual or corporate [income tax](/wiki/Income_tax "Income tax"). It also assesses no tax on retirement income earned and received from another state. Wyoming has a state [sales tax](/wiki/Sales_tax "Sales tax") of 4%. Counties have the option to collect an additional 1% tax for general revenue and a 1% tax for specific purposes, if approved by voters. Food for human consumption is not subject to sales tax.[[101]](#cite_note-104) A county lodging tax varies from 2% to 5%. The state collects a [use tax](/wiki/Use_tax "Use tax") of 5% on items purchased elsewhere and brought into Wyoming. All [property tax](/wiki/Property_tax "Property tax") is based on the property's assessed value; Wyoming's Department of Revenue's Ad Valorem Tax Division supports, trains, and guides local government agencies in the uniform assessment, valuation and taxation of locally assessed property. "Assessed value" means taxable value; "taxable value" means a percentage of the fair market value of property in a particular class. Statutes limit property tax increases. For county revenue, the property tax rate cannot exceed 12 [mills](/wiki/Mill_(currency) "Mill (currency)") (or 1.2%) of assessed value. For cities and towns, the rate is limited to eight mills (0.8%). With very few exceptions, state law limits the property tax rate for all governmental purposes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Personal property](/wiki/Personal_property "Personal property") held for personal use is tax-exempt. Inventory held for resale, pollution control equipment, cash, accounts receivable, stocks and bonds are also exempt. Other exemptions include property used for religious, educational, charitable, fraternal, benevolent and government purposes and improvements for handicapped access. Mine lands, underground mining equipment, and oil and gas extraction equipment are exempt from property tax, but companies must pay a gross products tax on minerals and a [severance tax](/wiki/Severance_tax "Severance tax") on mineral production.[[102]](#cite_note-105)[[103]](#cite_note-106)

Wyoming does not collect [capital gains tax](/wiki/Capital_gains_tax_in_the_United_States "Capital gains tax in the United States"), [gift tax](/wiki/Gift_tax_in_the_United_States "Gift tax in the United States"), or [estate tax](/wiki/Estate_tax_in_the_United_States "Estate tax in the United States").[[104]](#cite_note-107)

In 2008, the [Tax Foundation](/wiki/Tax_Foundation "Tax Foundation") reported that Wyoming had the most "business-friendly" tax climate of any U.S. state.[[105]](#cite_note-108) Wyoming state and local governments in fiscal year 2007 collected $2.242 billion in taxes, levies, and royalties from the oil and gas industry. The state's mineral industry, including oil, gas, [trona](/wiki/Trona "Trona"), and coal, provided $1.3 billion in property taxes from 2006 mineral production.[[91]](#cite_note-petro-94) As of 2017, Wyoming receives more federal tax dollars as a percentage of state general revenue than any state except [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana").[[106]](#cite_note-109)

As of 2016, Wyoming does not require the beneficial owners of [LLCs](/wiki/Limited_liability_company "Limited liability company") to be disclosed in the filing, which creates an opportunity for a tax haven, according to [Clark Stith](/wiki/Clark_Stith "Clark Stith") of Clark Stith & Associates.[[107]](#cite_note-Tax_Notes_Today-110) If fact, Wyoming was the first state to enact a statute authorizing the creation of LLCs.[[108]](#cite_note-Maynard-111) By 2024, company registrations were higher per capita in Wyoming than those in [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware"), which is historically the most prominent US tax haven.[[109]](#cite_note-:4-112) Entities linked to [foreign adversaries](/wiki/United_States_foreign_adversaries "United States foreign adversaries") have been observed exploiting Wyoming's business filing policies for fraudulent purposes, prompting state legislators to draft bills for increased oversight and restrictions. One of these bills, targeting foreign adversaries, was signed into law on February 24, 2025.[[110]](#cite_note-113)[[109]](#cite_note-:4-112)

### Transportation

Further information: [List of Wyoming railroads](/wiki/List_of_Wyoming_railroads "List of Wyoming railroads"), [List of airports in Wyoming](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Wyoming "List of airports in Wyoming"), and [List of state highways in Wyoming](/wiki/List_of_state_highways_in_Wyoming "List of state highways in Wyoming")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Major_roads_of_Wyoming%2C_July_24%2C_2025.png/250px-Major_roads_of_Wyoming%2C_July_24%2C_2025.png)](/wiki/File:Major_roads_of_Wyoming,_July_24,_2025.png)

Major highways of Wyoming

Wyoming's largest airport is [Jackson Hole Airport](/wiki/Jackson_Hole_Airport "Jackson Hole Airport"), with more than 500 employees.[[111]](#cite_note-114) Three interstate highways and 13 U.S. highways pass through Wyoming. The [Wyoming state highway system](/wiki/State_highways_in_Wyoming "State highways in Wyoming") also serves the state.

[Interstate 25](/wiki/Interstate_25_in_Wyoming "Interstate 25 in Wyoming") enters Wyoming south of Cheyenne and runs north, intersecting Interstate 80 immediately west of Cheyenne. It passes through Casper and ends at Interstate 90, near [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_Wyoming "Buffalo, Wyoming"). [Interstate 80](/wiki/Interstate_80_in_Wyoming "Interstate 80 in Wyoming") crosses the Utah border west of [Evanston](/wiki/Evanston,_Wyoming "Evanston, Wyoming") and runs east through the southern third of the state, passing through Cheyenne before entering Nebraska near [Pine Bluffs](/wiki/Pine_Bluffs,_Wyoming "Pine Bluffs, Wyoming"). [Interstate 90](/wiki/Interstate_90_in_Wyoming "Interstate 90 in Wyoming") comes into Wyoming near [Parkman](/wiki/Parkman,_Wyoming "Parkman, Wyoming") and cuts through the northeastern part of the state. It serves [Gillette](/wiki/Gillette,_Wyoming "Gillette, Wyoming") and enters South Dakota east of [Sundance](/wiki/Sundance,_Wyoming "Sundance, Wyoming").

U.S. Routes [14](/wiki/U.S._Route_14 "U.S. Route 14"), [16](/wiki/U.S._Route_16 "U.S. Route 16"), and [the eastern section of U.S. 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20#Eastern_Section "U.S. Route 20") have their western terminus at the eastern entrance to Yellowstone National Park and pass through [Cody](/wiki/Cody,_Wyoming "Cody, Wyoming"). U.S. 14 runs eastward before joining I-90 at [Gillette](/wiki/Gillette,_Wyoming "Gillette, Wyoming"). U.S. 14 then follows I-90 to the South Dakota border. U.S. 16 and 20 split off of U.S. 14 at [Greybull](/wiki/Greybull,_Wyoming "Greybull, Wyoming") and U.S. 16 turns east at [Worland](/wiki/Worland,_Wyoming "Worland, Wyoming") while U.S. 20 continues south [Shoshoni](/wiki/Shoshoni,_Wyoming "Shoshoni, Wyoming"). [U.S. Route 287](/wiki/U.S._Route_287 "U.S. Route 287") runs from [Fort Collins, Colorado](/wiki/Fort_Collins,_Colorado "Fort Collins, Colorado"), to [Laramie, Wyoming](/wiki/Laramie,_Wyoming "Laramie, Wyoming"), through a pass between the [Laramie Mountains](/wiki/Laramie_Mountains "Laramie Mountains") and the [Medicine Bow Mountains](/wiki/Medicine_Bow_Mountains "Medicine Bow Mountains"), then merges with US 30 and I-80 until it reaches Rawlins, where it continues north, passing Lander. Outside of [Moran](/wiki/Moran,_Wyoming "Moran, Wyoming"), U.S. 287 is part of a large interchange with U.S. Highways 26, 191, and 89, before continuing north to Yellowstone's southern entrance. U.S. 287 continues north of Yellowstone, but the park separates the two sections.

Other [U.S. highways](/wiki/United_States_Numbered_Highways "United States Numbered Highways") that pass through Wyoming are [18](/wiki/U.S._Highway_18_(Wyoming) "U.S. Highway 18 (Wyoming)"), [26](/wiki/U.S._Highway_26_(Wyoming) "U.S. Highway 26 (Wyoming)"), [30](/wiki/U.S._Highway_30_(Wyoming) "U.S. Highway 30 (Wyoming)"), [85](/wiki/U.S._Highway_85_(Wyoming) "U.S. Highway 85 (Wyoming)"), [87](/wiki/U.S._Highway_87_(Wyoming) "U.S. Highway 87 (Wyoming)"), [89](/wiki/U.S._Highway_89_(Wyoming) "U.S. Highway 89 (Wyoming)"), [189](/wiki/U.S._Highway_189_(Wyoming) "U.S. Highway 189 (Wyoming)"), [191](/wiki/U.S._Highway_191_(Wyoming) "U.S. Highway 191 (Wyoming)"), [212](/wiki/U.S._Highway_212_(Wyoming) "U.S. Highway 212 (Wyoming)"), and [287](/wiki/U.S._Highway_287_(Wyoming) "U.S. Highway 287 (Wyoming)").

Wyoming is one of only two states (the other is [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota")) in the [48 contiguous states](/wiki/48_contiguous_states "48 contiguous states") not served by [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak").[[112]](#cite_note-115) It was once served by Amtrak's [San Francisco Zephyr](/wiki/San_Francisco_Zephyr "San Francisco Zephyr") and [Pioneer](/wiki/Pioneer_(Amtrak) "Pioneer (Amtrak)") lines.[[113]](#cite_note-116) While no passenger trains roll through Wyoming today, intercity buses continue to connect residents across the state. Intercity bus carriers in the state include [Express Arrow](/w/index.php?title=Express_Arrow&action=edit&redlink=1 "Express Arrow (page does not exist)"), [Greyhound Lines](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines"), and [Jefferson Lines](/wiki/Jefferson_Lines "Jefferson Lines").[[114]](#cite_note-117)[[115]](#cite_note-118)[[116]](#cite_note-119)

#### Wind River Indian Reservation

Main article: [Wind River Indian Reservation](/wiki/Wind_River_Indian_Reservation "Wind River Indian Reservation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/WindRiverCanyon.JPG/250px-WindRiverCanyon.JPG)](/wiki/File:WindRiverCanyon.JPG)

[Wind River Canyon](/wiki/Wind_River_Canyon "Wind River Canyon")

The [Eastern Shoshone](/wiki/Shoshone "Shoshone") and [Northern Arapaho](/wiki/Arapaho "Arapaho") tribes share the Wind River [Indian Reservation](/wiki/Indian_reservation "Indian reservation") in central western Wyoming, near [Lander](/wiki/Lander,_Wyoming "Lander, Wyoming"). The reservation is home to 2,500 Eastern Shoshone and 5,000 Northern Arapaho.[[117]](#cite_note-120)

[Chief Washakie](/wiki/Chief_Washakie "Chief Washakie") established the reservation in 1868[[118]](#cite_note-shoshone-121) as the result of negotiations with the federal government in the [Fort Bridger](/wiki/Fort_Bridger "Fort Bridger") Treaty,[[119]](#cite_note-pbs-122) but the federal government forced the Northern Arapaho onto the Shoshone reservation in 1876 after it failed to provide a promised separate reservation.[[119]](#cite_note-pbs-122)

Today the Wind River Indian Reservation is jointly owned, with each tribe having a 50% interest in the land, water, and other natural resources.[[120]](#cite_note-arapaho-123) It is a sovereign, self-governed land with two independent governing bodies: the Eastern Shoshone Tribe and the Northern Arapaho Tribe. Until 2014, the Shoshone Business Council and Northern Arapaho Business Council met jointly as the Joint Business Council to decide matters that affect both tribes.[[118]](#cite_note-shoshone-121) Six elected council members from each tribe served on the joint council.

## Education

See also: [List of school districts in Wyoming](/wiki/List_of_school_districts_in_Wyoming "List of school districts in Wyoming") and [List of high schools in Wyoming](/wiki/List_of_high_schools_in_Wyoming "List of high schools in Wyoming")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Rocky_Mountain_Herbarium_University_of_Wyoming.JPG/250px-Rocky_Mountain_Herbarium_University_of_Wyoming.JPG)](/wiki/File:Rocky_Mountain_Herbarium_University_of_Wyoming.JPG)

The Rocky Mountain Herbarium at the University of Wyoming

The state superintendent of public instruction, an elected state official, directs [public education](/wiki/Public_education "Public education"). The State Board of Education, a nine-member board appointed by the governor, sets educational policy. The constitution prohibits the state from establishing curriculum and textbook selections; these are the prerogative of local school boards. The [Wyoming School for the Deaf](/wiki/Wyoming_School_for_the_Deaf "Wyoming School for the Deaf") was the only in-state school dedicated to supporting [deaf](/wiki/Deaf "Deaf") students before it closed in the summer of 2000.[[122]](#cite_note-125)

### Higher education

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of colleges and universities in Wyoming](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_Wyoming "List of colleges and universities in Wyoming").

Wyoming has a public four-year institution, the [University of Wyoming](/wiki/University_of_Wyoming "University of Wyoming") in [Laramie](/wiki/Laramie,_Wyoming "Laramie, Wyoming"), and a private four-year college, [Wyoming Catholic College](/wiki/Wyoming_Catholic_College "Wyoming Catholic College"), in [Lander](/wiki/Lander,_Wyoming "Lander, Wyoming"). There are also seven two-year [community colleges](/wiki/Community_college "Community college").

Before the passing of a new law in 2006, Wyoming had hosted unaccredited institutions, many of them suspected [diploma mills](/wiki/Diploma_mill "Diploma mill").[[123]](#cite_note-126) The 2006 law requires unaccredited institutions to make one of three choices: move out of Wyoming, close down, or apply for accreditation. The [Oregon State Office of Degree Authorization](/wiki/Oregon_State_Office_of_Degree_Authorization "Oregon State Office of Degree Authorization") predicted in 2007 that in a few years the problem of diploma mills in Wyoming might be resolved.[[124]](#cite_note-127)

### State government

Wyoming's Constitution established [three branches of government](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers"): the [executive](/wiki/List_of_Governors_of_Wyoming "List of Governors of Wyoming"), [legislative](/wiki/Wyoming_Legislature "Wyoming Legislature"), and [judicial](/wiki/Wyoming_Supreme_Court "Wyoming Supreme Court") branches. The [state legislature](/wiki/Wyoming_State_Legislature "Wyoming State Legislature") comprises a [House of Representatives](/wiki/Wyoming_House_of_Representatives "Wyoming House of Representatives") with 60 members and a [Senate](/wiki/Wyoming_Senate "Wyoming Senate") with 30 members. The executive branch is headed by the [governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Wyoming "Governor of Wyoming") and includes a [secretary of state](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_of_Wyoming "Secretary of State of Wyoming"), [auditor](/wiki/Wyoming_State_Auditor "Wyoming State Auditor"), treasurer, and superintendent of public instruction. As Wyoming does not have a [lieutenant governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_governor_(United_States)#Wyoming "Lieutenant governor (United States)"), the secretary of state is first in the line of succession.

Wyoming's sparse population warrants the state only one [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large") seat in the [U.S. House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"), and hence only three votes in the [Electoral College](/wiki/Electoral_College_(United_States) "Electoral College (United States)").

The Wyoming State Liquor Association is the state's sole legal wholesale distributor of spirits, making it an [alcoholic beverage control state](/wiki/Alcoholic_beverage_control_state "Alcoholic beverage control state"). With the exception of wine, state law prohibits the purchase of alcoholic beverages for resale from any other source.[[130]](#cite_note-WYlaws-133)

### Judicial system

Wyoming's highest court is the [Supreme Court of Wyoming](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Wyoming "Supreme Court of Wyoming"), with five justices presiding over appeals from the state's lower courts. Wyoming is unusual in that it does not have an intermediate [appellate court](/wiki/Appellate_court "Appellate court"), like most states. This is largely attributable to the state's population and correspondingly lower caseload. Appeals from the state district courts go directly to the Wyoming Supreme Court. Wyoming also has state circuit courts (formerly county courts), of limited jurisdiction, which handle certain types of cases, such as civil claims with lower dollar amounts, misdemeanor criminal offenses, and [felony](/wiki/Felony "Felony") [arraignments](/wiki/Arraignment "Arraignment"). Circuit court judges also commonly hear small claims cases as well.

Before 1972, Wyoming judges were selected by popular vote on a nonpartisan ballot. This earlier system was criticized by the state bar which called for the adoption of the [Missouri Plan](/wiki/Missouri_Plan "Missouri Plan"), a system designed to balance judiciary independence with judiciary accountability. In 1972, an amendment to Article 5 of the [Wyoming Constitution](/wiki/Wyoming_Constitution "Wyoming Constitution"), which incorporated a modified version of the plan, was adopted by the voters. Since the adoption of the amendment, all state court judges in Wyoming are nominated by the Judicial Nominating Commission and appointed by the Governor. They are then subject to a [retention vote](/wiki/Retention_vote "Retention vote") by the electorate one year after appointment.[[131]](#cite_note-134)

Wyoming's political history defies easy classification. The state was the first to grant women the right to vote and to elect a woman governor.[[133]](#cite_note-Today_in_History-136) On December 10, 1869, [John Allen Campbell](/wiki/John_Allen_Campbell "John Allen Campbell"), the first Governor of the Wyoming Territory, approved the first law in United States history explicitly granting women the right to vote. This day was later commemorated as Wyoming Day.[[133]](#cite_note-Today_in_History-136) On November 5, 1889, voters approved the first constitution in the world granting full voting rights to women.[[133]](#cite_note-Today_in_History-136)

While the state elected notable [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") to federal office in the 1960s and 1970s, politics have become decidedly more conservative since the 1980s as the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") came to dominate the state's congressional delegation. Today, Wyoming is represented in Washington by its two Senators, [John Barrasso](/wiki/John_Barrasso "John Barrasso") and [Cynthia Lummis](/wiki/Cynthia_Lummis "Cynthia Lummis"), and its one member of the House of Representatives, Congresswoman [Harriet Hageman](/wiki/Harriet_Hageman "Harriet Hageman"). All three are Republicans; a Democrat has not represented Wyoming in the Senate since 1977 or in the House since 1978. The state has not voted for a Democrat for president since 1964, one of only eight times since statehood. In the 2004 presidential election, [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") won his second-largest victory, with 69% of the vote. Former Vice President [Dick Cheney](/wiki/Dick_Cheney "Dick Cheney") was a Wyoming resident and represented the state in Congress from 1979 to 1989.

The last time a Democrat won a statewide election in Wyoming was in [2006](/wiki/2006_Wyoming_gubernatorial_election "2006 Wyoming gubernatorial election"), when Democratic governor [Dave Freudenthal](/wiki/Dave_Freudenthal "Dave Freudenthal") was re-elected to a second term by a wide margin, winning every county in the state. For 19 of Wyoming's 23 counties, 2006 marked the last time that they voted for the Democratic nominee in a statewide race. Of the remaining 4, [Sweetwater County](/wiki/Sweetwater_County "Sweetwater County") last voted Democratic in the [2008 U.S. House race](/wiki/2008_United_States_House_of_Representatives_election_in_Wyoming "2008 United States House of Representatives election in Wyoming") and [Laramie County](/wiki/Laramie_County "Laramie County") last voted Democratic in the [2014 Superintendent of Public Instruction race](/wiki/2014_Wyoming_elections#Superintendent_of_Public_Instruction "2014 Wyoming elections"), leaving [Teton](/wiki/Teton_County,_Wyoming "Teton County, Wyoming") and [Albany](/wiki/Albany_County,_Wyoming "Albany County, Wyoming") as the only counties that Democrats are able to win. Teton, which is composed of affluent resort communities, is reliably Democratic, except in Republican landslides like the [2022 gubernatorial election](/wiki/2022_Wyoming_gubernatorial_election "2022 Wyoming gubernatorial election"); Albany, which contains the [college town](/wiki/College_town "College town") of [Laramie](/wiki/Laramie,_Wyoming "Laramie, Wyoming"), is more competitive.

Republicans are dominant at the state level. They have held a majority in the state senate continuously since 1936 and in the state house since 1964, though Democrats held the [governorship](/wiki/Governor_of_Wyoming "Governor of Wyoming") for all but eight years between 1975 and 2011. Uniquely, Wyoming elected Democrat [Nellie Tayloe Ross](/wiki/Nellie_Tayloe_Ross "Nellie Tayloe Ross") as the first woman in United States history to serve as state governor. She served from 1925 to 1927, winning a special election after her husband, [William Bradford Ross](/wiki/William_Bradford_Ross "William Bradford Ross"), unexpectedly died a little more than a year into his term.[[134]](#cite_note-137)

Wyoming retains the [death penalty](/wiki/Death_penalty "Death penalty"). Authorized methods of execution include the [gas chamber](/wiki/Gas_chamber "Gas chamber").[[135]](#cite_note-138)

## Culture

### Sports

See also: [List of college athletic programs in Wyoming](/wiki/List_of_college_athletic_programs_in_Wyoming "List of college athletic programs in Wyoming")

Due to its sparse population, Wyoming lacks any major professional sports teams; the [Gillette Mustangs](/wiki/Gillette_Mustangs "Gillette Mustangs"), an [indoor football](/wiki/Indoor_American_football "Indoor American football") team based in Gillette that began play in 2021 prior to their departure from the city in 2023, were previously the only professional team in the state. However, the [Wyoming Cowboys and Cowgirls](/wiki/Wyoming_Cowboys_and_Cowgirls "Wyoming Cowboys and Cowgirls")—particularly the [football](/wiki/Wyoming_Cowboys_football "Wyoming Cowboys football") and basketball teams—are quite popular; their stadiums in Laramie are about 7,200 feet (2,200 m) above sea level, the highest in [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I"). The [Wyoming High School Activities Association](/wiki/Wyoming_High_School_Activities_Association "Wyoming High School Activities Association") also sponsors twelve sports and there are three [junior ice hockey](/wiki/Junior_ice_hockey "Junior ice hockey") teams, all of which are members of the [NA3HL](/wiki/North_American_3_Hockey_League "North American 3 Hockey League"). [Casper](/wiki/Casper,_Wyoming "Casper, Wyoming") has hosted the [College National Finals Rodeo](/wiki/College_National_Finals_Rodeo "College National Finals Rodeo") since 2001.
